(i).question: Compare and contrast the cover provided by critical illness (CI) and income protection (IP) insurance policies. [6]

Comparison of CI and IP: Both IP and CI insurances provide cash benefits rather than an indemnity for the policyholder.  [½]
IP seeks to indemnify policyholders against loss of income while they are prevented from working because of injury or illness.    [½]
It is not a true indemnity product because the benefit is restricted to the minimum of the benefit chosen by the policyholder and a limit (eg 60% of pre‐sickness income) imposed by the insurer. [½]
The policyholder will always suffer some loss as a result of the insured event.  This acts to reduce the likelihood of fraudulent claims (moral hazard).    [½]
IP insurance provides a regular income from the end of the deferred period until the policyholder returns to work or the policy term ends, whichever event occurs first.    [½]
CI insurance provides a fixed lump sum monetary benefit (chosen by the policyholder), usually irrespective of the loss incurred by the policyholder, as a result of the insured event occurring.  [½]
Often IP benefit payments (and premiums) may be linked to an index of wage or price inflation.   [½]
CI benefits (and premiums) may also be linked to an inflation index. [½]
IP insurance provides cover for repeated occurrences of the insured event – the inability to earn a ‘normal’ wage – during the term of the policy.   [½]
The insured event is specified in terms of outcomes (inability to work normally) rather than causes (illnesses or accident).      [½]
For CI insurance, the insured event is usually specified in terms of causes, eg a list of illnesses or medical conditions that lead to the payment of the sum insured.    [½]
Therefore the cover is much more restricted than that provided by an IP policy.    [½]
For example, a broken leg or a stress‐related illness might lead to an IP payment, but not to a CI payment. [½]
However, sometimes CI cover is extended by the use of an outcome event, eg total permanent disablement, whatever the cause.    [½]
IP cover (and premiums) do not cease until the death of the policyholder or the end of the term. [½]
However, premiums may be waived during periods when benefits are being paid. [½]
CI insurance provides cover for a single occurrence of the insured event.    [½]
Once the sum insured has been paid the cover (and premium) ceases.    [½]
CI insurance could be seen as an accelerated form of life assurance, whereas IP insurance is more like an ill‐health pre‐retirement pension. [½]
[Maximum 6]

(ii).question: Describe the types of individual for which each of CI and IP insurance policies might be suitable. [3]

Types of individuals who should buy each: The type of individual who should buy each policy is the person who has residual risks (after taking into account all the other benefits to which they are entitled), that match the risks insured by that policy.    [1]

An employed person whose employer provides a comprehensive sick pay scheme (eg paying a large proportion of normal salary for the first 12 or 24 months of sickness), together with an occupational pension scheme that includes an ill‐health retirement pension, will have little need for IP insurance.    [1]
However, there may be a gap between the time at which sick pay ceases and an ill‐health retirement pension begins.    [½]
In addition, an IP policy would provide cover for long‐term absence from work that was not sufficient to justify retirement on grounds of ill health.    [½]
On the other hand, a self‐employed person would have an immediate need for IP insurance, irrespective of whether they had dependants.    [½]
In general, CI insurance will be suitable for anyone who would consider a lump sum payment useful should they become critically ill, … [½]
…whether for repaying debts, replacing lost income, or paying for care or treatment.    [½]
Someone with IP insurance or equivalent income protection may still have a need for CI insurance, though arguably to a lesser extent as the uses of the benefit partly overlap. [½]
In countries where there is no comprehensive free medical care, people often use the CI sum insured to pay for the treatment needed as a result of the CI. [½]

2.2.label: Question 2.2
2.2.scenario: In recent times, health services have increased the use of screening programmes with the aim of detecting diseases at an early stage in their development when they can be treated more effectively.  Examples are screening programs for breast cancer, cervical cancer and prostate cancer.
2.2.question: Describe the possible impact on CI claim costs of these developments.

Impact of screening on CI costs: CI has a fixed sum insured and it is only the incidence rates that will affect claims costs. [½]
Effective screening is likely to detect more incidents, but with a greater proportion of these being at an earlier stage of the disease than in the past. [1]
In general, this will tend to increase claim incidence rates and so increase costs.    [1]
However, some of the incidents detected at an early stage may not qualify as claims, eg cancers that have not invaded adjacent tissues. [1]

As a secondary effect, screening may lead to the development of effective treatments that considerably reduce the adverse effects of the insured condition, and result in more windfall claims (ie claims where the sum insured is paid, but the policyholder suffers little or no financial or other loss). [1]

2.3.label: Question 2.3
2.3.scenario: Your company’s CI policies currently provide cover for children up to age 18.  A claim as a result of a child’s illness does not terminate the policy.  The marketing manager has suggested that this benefit costs very little and has a high perceived value among policyholders.  She suggests that current and future policies should be revised so that the child’s benefit is available until the age of 18 or the end of full‐time education if this is later, but that premiums should remain unchanged.
2.3.question: Comment on this suggestion. [4]

Comment on Marketing Suggestion: The key issue is whether the expected additional claims costs will be covered by other sources of additional income.    [½]
Additional claims costs: The increased claims costs will depend on the:  incidence rates for CIs    [½]
Incidence rates for CIs for children over the age of 18 but in full‐time education should be investigated, but are likely to be low.  So the additional costs are likely to be small. [½]
Therefore the most important factor is likely to be the sum insured offered on a child.    [½]
If this is the full sum insured, then the costs might be significant and there may be a temptation for proposers to select against the insurer … [½]
… because underwriting for cover will be on the policyholder’s life rather than the child’s life.   [½]
So if it is not already a policy condition, limiting the sum insured on a child to, say, 25% of the main sum insured or making it a fixed sum of a similar magnitude would be sensible. [½]
Additional income: This change will increase the ‘quality’ of the product.    [½]
The additional benefit should be easily understood by policyholders and may prove attractive, … [½]
… particularly if sales are predominantly to the higher social classes where children generally remain dependent on their parents to higher ages than in the lower social classes. [½]
The additional income might therefore come from increased volume of sales that will reduce the fixed costs per policy.    [½]
